B.Cyberbullyingfrequentlydominates theheadlines ofnewspapers. C. Young people should be encouraged to take action when bullied online. D. Victims of cyberbullying suffer the same as those of face-to-face bullying.

options.C: Young people should be encouraged to take action when bullied online.
options.D: Victims of cyberbullying suffer the same as those of face-to-face bullying.

questions.passage_text: Read the following passage about an opinion on cyberbullying and mark the letter A,B,C,or D to indicatethecorrect answertoeachof thequestionsfrom6to13. Cyberbullying hasbeen exaggerated according toresearch byDr Olweus of theUniversity of Bergen. His survey reports that 18% of students have been bullied but only 5% of bullying occurs online. It also tells us that face-to-face bullying is still the way most people are bullied. Surprisingly, cyberbullying has all the newspaper headlines, giving the impression of a massive problem that is damaging our children's mental health. I'mnotsaying thatcyberbullyingdoesnothavenegative consequences.Targetsof cyberbullying,like thetargets offace-to-facebullying,sufferfrom anxiety,depression andself-harm. For me,cyberbullying is simply a new addition to an old problem, so our schools can deal with cyberbullying within their existing anti-bullying strategies.I think parents alsohave a responsibility for teaching their children toignore nasty messages and not be oversensitive. Young people also need the confidence to use the block or report button if they receive abusive communication online.Hopefully,by learning how to neutralise an online bully,they will learn valuable life skills for managing any unpleasant people they meet in their future studies and work. (AdaptedfromC21Smart) Vi quyen lgi chinh dang cua chinh cac em, TUYET DOI KHONG chia se tai lieu DOc QUYEN nay cho nguroi khac! Dang Ky Khoa H c Online T iFanpage: Tai Li u Khoa H c UniMap

questions.question_text: .grants A.where whose D.whom B,that Question5. ReadthefollowingpassageaboutdifficultjobsandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answertoeachofthequestionsfrom6to14. Jeff Haslam spends much of his time working underground in London's sewers.These tunnels are crucial for transporting wastewater and other refuse away from the city. Jeff's main job is to keep these clean."We work in teams of six,"he explains,noting the dirty and dark conditions of their work. He wears specialised clothing, a mask, and a helmet with a light to navigate these challenging environments. In contrast, Charlie Radley works as a fisherman in the cold waters of Alaska. Each fishing trip lasts about a week, and the weather can be extremely harsh.“It's often stormy and very cold,” Charlie says,highlighting the need for warm,waterproof clothing. While he has the potential to earn a good income,it greatly depends on the success of their catch. If they don't bring in enough fish, his earnings significantly drop. SandySmith is a lumberjack who enjoysworking outdoors.She noticed a job advertisement for lumberjacks online and decided to apply, despite the long hours and modest pay. "It's a very dangerous job," Sandy admits,emphasising the risks associated with using heavy machinery in remote areas. She is always cautious, as medicalfacilities are always far away. Despitetheirdifferentoccupations,Jeff,Charlie,andSandyallfacesignificantchallengesin theirjobs.Each onedealswithuniquedifficulties,frommaintaining sewersandfishinginharsh conditions to cutting down trees.Their storiesillustrate the dedication required for roles that are essential to society,even if they often go unrecognised. (AdaptedfromSolutions) Vi quyen lqi chinh dang cua chinh cac em. TUYET DOl KHONG chia se tai lieu DOc QuYEN nay cho nguoi khac! Dang Ky Khoa H c Online T i Fanpage: Tai Li u Khoa H c UniMap

of applicants.They tend tofavour the candidates with more employability skills-the skills necessary for

Interpersonal skills arereally crucialwhenseekingemployment.They aretheskillsthatpeople
use tointeractwith each other.Peoplewith good interpersonal skillsoften cooperate andworkwell with
the others in a team. They are also good at negotiating and satisfying the expectations of the people
involved.
Communication skills are alsoimportant for people who are seeking employment or looking for a

of getting and keeping the job they want.

Communication skills are alsoimportant for people who are seeking employment or looking for a promotionintheir current careerbecause employersnowadayswant tofindemployeeswhocommunicate wellbothverballyandinwritingwithawidevarietyofpeople.Goodcommunicatorscanspeakandwrite clearly and concisely, getting their messages across with less chance of misunderstanding. It is hard to get a job. It is even harder to keep it, succeed in it, and gain a promotion. However, if peopleknowhowtointeract properly and communicatewell with others,they can increase their chance of getting and keeping the job they want. (AdaptedfromEnglish12Workbook) Vi quyen lqi chinh dang cua chinh cac em. TUYET DOl KHONG chia se tai lieu DOc QuYEN nay cho nguoi khac! Dang Ky Khoa H c Online T i Fanpage: Tai Li u Khoa H c UniMap

questions.question_text: B.respect Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of the questions from 6 to 13. Zoos are hugely popular attractions for adults and children alike.But are they actually a good thing? Critics of zoos would argue that animals often suffer physically and mentally by being enclosed. Even the best artificial environments can't come close to matching the space, diversity,and freedom that animals have in their natural habitats.This deprivation causes many zoo animals to become stressed or mentally ill. On the other hand, by bringing people and animals together, zoos have the potential to educate the public about conservation issues and inspire people to protect animals and their habitats. One of the most important modern functions of zoos is supporting international breeding programmes, particularly for endangered species. In the wild, some of the rarest species have difficulty in finding mates and breeding, and they might also be threatened by poachers, loss of their habitat and predators.A good zoowill enable these species tolive andbreed in a secure environment.However, opponentsofzoossaythatthevastmajorityofcaptivebreedingprogrammesdonotreleaseanimals back into the wild. Vi quyen lqi chinh dang cua chinh cac em. TUYET DOl KHONG chia se tai lieu DOc QuYEN nay cho nguoi khac! Dang Ky Khoa H c Online T i Fanpage: Tai Li u Khoa H c UniMap

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a new expectation in the gig economy? B. Career promotion opportunities
questions.passage_text: Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B,C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of thequestionsfrom1to8. The rise of the gig economy has transformed how millions of people think about work, stability, and personal choice. As traditional full-time jobs become harder to secure in many cities, more individuals areturning toshort-term,flexible opportunities toearn anincome.This shift affects not onlyworkers but also companies and communities that must adapt to new expectations about work hours,responsibility, andlong-termcommitment. For example, Linh, a 27-year-old graphic designer in Ho Chi Minh City, once worked in a small advertising agency. After experiencing constant overtime and limited career growth, she left to become a freelance designer. Today, she accepts projects through online platforms, from creating brand logos to designing social media content for international clients.Linh enjoys thefreedom to select the jobs that interest her, organise her schedule, and negotiate her own rates. However, she also admits that the income can be unpredictable,and she must manage everything—from marketing to customer service—on her own. The gig economy offers significant advantages.Workers like Linh can develop a broad skill set, build global networks, and enjoy a sense of autonomy rarely found in traditional roles. Companies, meanwhile,benefit from accessing skilled workers without long-term contracts,allowing them to respond quicklytomarketchanges.Manygovernmentshavealsorecognisedthegigeconomy'spotential to boostinnovationandcreateopportunitiesforyoungpeople. Still, the model presents serious challenges. Gig workers often lack health insurance, paid leave, and legalprotection,leavingthemvulnerableduringillnessoreconomicdownturns.Competition ondigital platforms can also be intense, pushing prices down and reducing job security.As the gig economy continues to expand, policymakers must decide how to balance flexibility with fairness, ensuring that people like Linh can pursue meaningful work without sacrificing financial stability or prospects.

questions.passage_text: Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B,C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of thequestionsfrom1to8. The rise of the gig economy has transformed how millions of people think about work, stability, and personal choice. As traditional full-time jobs become harder to secure in many cities, more individuals areturning toshort-term,flexible opportunities toearn anincome.This shift affects not onlyworkers but also companies and communities that must adapt to new expectations about work hours,responsibility, andlong-termcommitment. For example, Linh, a 27-year-old graphic designer in Ho Chi Minh City, once worked in a small advertising agency. After experiencing constant overtime and limited career growth, she left to become a freelance designer. Today, she accepts projects through online platforms, from creating brand logos to designing social media content for international clients.Linh enjoys thefreedom to select the jobs that interest her, organise her schedule, and negotiate her own rates. However, she also admits that the income can be unpredictable,and she must manage everything—from marketing to customer service—on her own. The gig economy offers significant advantages.Workers like Linh can develop a broad skill set, build global networks, and enjoy a sense of autonomy rarely found in traditional roles. Companies, meanwhile,benefit from accessing skilled workers without long-term contracts,allowing them to respond quicklytomarketchanges.Manygovernmentshavealsorecognisedthegigeconomy'spotential to boostinnovationandcreateopportunitiesforyoungpeople. Still, the model presents serious challenges. Gig workers often lack health insurance, paid leave, and legalprotection,leavingthemvulnerableduringillnessoreconomicdownturns.Competition ondigital platforms can also be intense, pushing prices down and reducing job security.As the gig economy continues to expand, policymakers must decide how to balance flexibility with fairness, ensuring that people like Linh can pursue meaningful work without sacrificing financial stability or prospects.

questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co Vu Thi Mai Phghaged By Funpage:drairhfttakHoanganhaiNiphuong.vn TAILIEUDOCQUYEN DIKEMKHOAHOC TU'DUY TUYEN TINH (LINEARTHINKING) Docquyenva duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Chinhphuccauhoi10diem CoVu Thi Mai Phuo'ng Read thefollowingpassageandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrectanswertoeachof thequestionsfrom1to8. StephenWiltshire is aBritish artist admired for his astonishing ability to draw detailed cityscapes entirelyfrom memory. He was born in London in 1974 toWest Indian parents and diagnosed with autism at the age of three.As a young child, he hardly spoke and often seemed withdrawn from the people around him. Yet, while words were difficult for him, he found comfort in sketching cars, animals, and buildings.His teachers quickly noticed that his pictures contained remarkable detail and perspective. When Stephen was eight, he received his first major commission to draw Salisbury Cathedral, and that opportunity introduced him to the public.Later,at the City and Guilds of London Art School,he developed his technique and gained a deeper understanding of architecture.His talent is extraordinary: after observing a city once, often from a helicopter, he can reproduce its skyline with incredible accuracy. His panoramic drawings of NewYork,Tokyo, and Rome capture not only every building but also the rhythm ofurbanlife,as if the citiesbreathe through hispencil. Today, Stephen's works are exhibited internationally, and he manages his own gallery in London's Royal Opera Arcade. His art has been purchased by collectors, featured in documentaries, and praised by critics who call him“the human camera.”In 2006,he was awarded the MBE for his services to art. StephenWiltshire continuestotravel,observe,and draw.Hesays that art allowshimto connect deeply with people and places.Each new work reflects his sharp memory, patient technique, and unique way of seeing the world — transforming everyday landscapes into vivid expressions of life and emotion. Question1.What is NOT mentioned inparagraph1 as somethingStephenWiltshire liked to draw? A.buildings D.animals B. words C. cars

questions.relative_path: MP/LUYỆN GIẢI ĐỀ THI TỐT NGHIỆP/CHINH PHỤC CÂU HỎI 10 ĐIỂM/LINEAR THINKING/8.plus-2-luyen-tap-ung-dung-chuyen-sau-tu-duy-tuyen-tinh-buoi-3.pdf
questions.question_text: The word "meet”" in paragraph 1 can be best replaced by
questions.passage_text: Adolescence is widely recognised as one of the most transformative and demanding stages of life. Teenagers must navigate rapid physical and emotional changes while trying tomeet academic expectations and maintain social connections. This period often brings a mixture of excitement and uncertainty, as young people attempt to understand themselves and their place in an increasingly complex world. As a result, many teens experience internal pressure that is difficult to communicate openly. One of the most prominent challenges is the influence of digital culture. Social media platforms allow teenagers to express creativity and stay connected, but they also expose them to constant comparison. Curated images, edited videos, and idealised lifestyles can quietly affect how teens evaluate their own achievements and appearance. The fear of missing out, along with the desire for online validation, can intensify feelings of anxiety or self-criticism. Over time, these pressures may shape how young people perceive success,happiness, and personal worth. Another significantissueinvolvesthestruggletoformastableidentity.Teenagersmustmake decisions about future careers, relationships, and personal values at a time when their minds are still developing. Conflicts may arisewhen their interests_do not align with the expectations of parents, teachers,or peers.This tension can lead to frustration or confusion as teens attempt to define who they about the future, especially in fast-changing societies. Supporting teenagers requires consistentguidance and empatheticcommunication.Schools can play a vital role by promoting emotional resilience and teaching practical coping strategies. Families, meanwhile, can help by listening without judgement and encouraging healthy routines. When adults acknowledge the complexity of teen issues and offer reliable support, young people gain the confidence and stability they need to navigate this pivotal stage and move towards adulthood with greater clarity and strength.

questions.question_text: The word “they”in paragraph 3 refers to B. container farms
questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co Vu Thi Mai PhShatedEBy Fanpage:dTairbitutaK hrenbaolcoiMighuong.vn TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHQC TU'DUYTUYENTINH(LINEARTHINKING) LUYENTAPCHUYENSAU TU'DUY TUYENTINH(BUOI4) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Chinhphuccauhoi10diem Co Vu Thi Mai Phuong Read thefollowingpassageandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answertoeachof the questions from 1 to8. in the most unexpected of places.Abandoned urban lots,industrial zones,and even rooftops are now host to a new form of farming. This innovative approach utilises repurposed shipping containers, transforming these steel boxes into highly controlled environments for growing food. This is container farming, a methodthat challenges our traditionalperceptionsof agriculture andbrings thefarm directlytothe consumer. The interior of a container farm is a world away from an open field. Every aspect of the environment is meticulously managed. Instead of soil, plants grow vertically in nutrient-rich water solutions or mist, a technique known as hydroponics or aeroponics. LED lights, tuned to specific wavelengths, replace the sun,providing a consistent and optimal light source for photosynthesis. Advanced climate control systems maintain the perfect temperature and humidity, creating an eternal summer inside the steel walls, completelyindependentoftheweatheroutside. This controlled environment offers significant advantages. Container farms can produce yields vastlyhigher than traditional farming on the same footprint,all while using afraction of thewater. Because they arelocated within urban centres,they drastically reduce the distancefood travelsfromfarm to plate, enhancing freshness and nutritional value. Furthermore,growing food indoors eliminates the needfor chemical pesticides,resulting in cleaner,healthier produce.This system provides a reliable harvestyear-round,unaffectedby droughts,floods,orseasonal changes. The implications of this technology are substantial. Container farming presents a powerful solution to increase food security in cities and reduce the environmental impact of agriculture. It allows for the local production of freshgreens in areas wherefertile soil is scarce or contaminated.While it may not replace vast field crops like wheat or corn, it represents a crucial step towards more resilient and sustainablefood systems,proving that thefuture offarmingmightjust befound inside a simplesteel box.

questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co Vu Thi Mai PhShatedEBy Fanpage:dTairbitutaK hrenbaolcoiMighuong.vn TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHQC TU'DUYTUYENTINH(LINEARTHINKING) LUYENTAPCHUYENSAU TU'DUY TUYENTINH(BUOI4) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Chinhphuccauhoi10diem Co Vu Thi Mai Phuong Read thefollowingpassageandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answertoeachof the questions from 1 to8. in the most unexpected of places.Abandoned urban lots,industrial zones,and even rooftops are now host to a new form of farming. This innovative approach utilises repurposed shipping containers, transforming these steel boxes into highly controlled environments for growing food. This is container farming, a methodthat challenges our traditionalperceptionsof agriculture andbrings thefarm directlytothe consumer. The interior of a container farm is a world away from an open field. Every aspect of the environment is meticulously managed. Instead of soil, plants grow vertically in nutrient-rich water solutions or mist, a technique known as hydroponics or aeroponics. LED lights, tuned to specific wavelengths, replace the sun,providing a consistent and optimal light source for photosynthesis. Advanced climate control systems maintain the perfect temperature and humidity, creating an eternal summer inside the steel walls, completelyindependentoftheweatheroutside. This controlled environment offers significant advantages. Container farms can produce yields vastlyhigher than traditional farming on the same footprint,all while using afraction of thewater. Because they arelocated within urban centres,they drastically reduce the distancefood travelsfromfarm to plate, enhancing freshness and nutritional value. Furthermore,growing food indoors eliminates the needfor chemical pesticides,resulting in cleaner,healthier produce.This system provides a reliable harvestyear-round,unaffectedby droughts,floods,orseasonal changes. The implications of this technology are substantial. Container farming presents a powerful solution to increase food security in cities and reduce the environmental impact of agriculture. It allows for the local production of freshgreens in areas wherefertile soil is scarce or contaminated.While it may not replace vast field crops like wheat or corn, it represents a crucial step towards more resilient and sustainablefood systems,proving that thefuture offarmingmightjust befound inside a simplesteel box.

Question 18.Which of the following best summarises the passage?
A. Inspired by simple Thai heaters, the Desolenator is a portable, solar-powered device that purifies
various water sources,offering a sustainable solution to global water scarcity.
B. William Janssen developed the Desolenator after witnessing issues in the UAE,aiming to provide a
basic water supply for a family's daily needs worldwide.
C.The Desolenator is a compact desalination unit that uses a solar panel and battery to heat water into
steam before condensing it, yielding fifteen litres daily.
D. Due to severe water stress affecting two-thirds of the population, Janssen urges governments of
developed countries to purchase the Desolenator toreplace expensive desalination plants.
JUNIMAP
KHOAHOC
Ihl

questions.question_text: The phrase“this practice”in paragraph 2 refers to
questions.passage_text: Read the following passage and mark theletter A,B,C,or D to indicate thecorrect answer to each of the questions from 1 to8. In numerous agricultural regions across the globe, the practice of crop burning remains a common, yet deeply problematic, method for clearing fields after the harvest.Farmers set fire to the leftover straw and stubble torapidly eliminate debris and prepare theland for thenext plantingcycle.Thistechniqueis often perceived as the most expedient and cost-effective solution, requiring minimal labour and machinery. Despite its immediate practical benefits for the farming community, this short-sighted approach generates a cascade of severe environmental and health repercussions that extend far beyond the field's edge. The most immediate and visible consequence of this practice is the dense,noxious smog that blankets vast areas, drastically diminishing air quality. The smoke releases a hazardous cocktail of particulate matter and toxic gases, including carbon monoxide and volatile organic compounds. For local populations, this leads to a surge in respiratory ailments such as asthma and bronchitis, and poses a particular threat to the most vulnerable members of society, including children and the elderly. The resulting haze can also obscure visibility, creating dangerous conditions for transportation on roads and LIEU-KHOAHOC at airports. Beyond the palpable smog, the environmental degradation is profound. Crop burning is a significant substantial volumes of carbon dioxide and methane.Furthermore,the intense heat from the fires devastates thesoil'secosystem,incineratingessential nutrients andbeneficial microorganisms.Thisleads tolong-term soil infertility,undermining theveryfoundation of sustainable agriculture.The practice also causes irreparable harm to local biodiversity, destroying habitats and killing small animals and insects crucial to ahealthy environment. Addressingthisdeeplyentrenchedissuerequiresaconcertedeffortandthepromotionof viable alternatives. Governments and agricultural agencies must provide education and financial incorporating theresiduebackinto the soil toenhance its organic content,or even convertingthewaste into biofuel offer economically and ecologically sound solutions. Transitioning away from crop burning is not merely an environmental imperative but a crucial investment in public health and the long-term viability ofour agricultural systems.

A. crop burning

questions.passage_text: Read the following passage and mark theletter A,B,C,or D to indicate thecorrect answer to each of the questions from 1 to8. In numerous agricultural regions across the globe, the practice of crop burning remains a common, yet deeply problematic, method for clearing fields after the harvest.Farmers set fire to the leftover straw and stubble torapidly eliminate debris and prepare theland for thenext plantingcycle.Thistechniqueis often perceived as the most expedient and cost-effective solution, requiring minimal labour and machinery. Despite its immediate practical benefits for the farming community, this short-sighted approach generates a cascade of severe environmental and health repercussions that extend far beyond the field's edge. The most immediate and visible consequence of this practice is the dense,noxious smog that blankets vast areas, drastically diminishing air quality. The smoke releases a hazardous cocktail of particulate matter and toxic gases, including carbon monoxide and volatile organic compounds. For local populations, this leads to a surge in respiratory ailments such as asthma and bronchitis, and poses a particular threat to the most vulnerable members of society, including children and the elderly. The resulting haze can also obscure visibility, creating dangerous conditions for transportation on roads and LIEU-KHOAHOC at airports. Beyond the palpable smog, the environmental degradation is profound. Crop burning is a significant substantial volumes of carbon dioxide and methane.Furthermore,the intense heat from the fires devastates thesoil'secosystem,incineratingessential nutrients andbeneficial microorganisms.Thisleads tolong-term soil infertility,undermining theveryfoundation of sustainable agriculture.The practice also causes irreparable harm to local biodiversity, destroying habitats and killing small animals and insects crucial to ahealthy environment. Addressingthisdeeplyentrenchedissuerequiresaconcertedeffortandthepromotionof viable alternatives. Governments and agricultural agencies must provide education and financial incorporating theresiduebackinto the soil toenhance its organic content,or even convertingthewaste into biofuel offer economically and ecologically sound solutions. Transitioning away from crop burning is not merely an environmental imperative but a crucial investment in public health and the long-term viability ofour agricultural systems.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 4? "Byconsciouslydifferentiatingbetweeninspirationaladvocacyandmerepromotionalcontent, individuals can mitigatenegativecomparisons." Nosooner had individuals questioned theintent behindwhat they see than theybegan tolimit the pressure created by these comparisons. When people learn to tell true advocacy apart from simple promotion, they can reduce unhelpful self- comparison. Were people to focus less on the purpose of online messages, they might lessen some of the self- imposed comparisons. By paying attention to how influencers frame their content, individuals can ease certain feelings of pressure without fully resolving them. ReadingPassage2 For nearly three decades, the world's nations have convened in grand summits, tasked with forging a unified front against global warming.These gatherings, often culminating in late-night negotiations and sweepingdeclarations,project animageofinternational solidarity.The2015Paris Agreementstands as their landmark achievement,a delicate consensus tolimit temperature rise.Yet,behind the thetangibleactionrequiredbeyond them. The core failure lies in the chasm between ambition and implementation.While targets are set collectively,theresponsibilityfor enacting themfalls toindividual nations,eachwithits own political pressures and economic priorities. Commitments often rely on future technologies or distant deadlines, allowing short-term interests to override long-term necessity.Consequently, annual emissions continue their upward trajectory, rendering the meticulously negotiated goals increasingly theoretical. The summitshavebecome adept at setting destinations but lack the authority tomap the compulsory journey. This cycle generates a deep-seated cynicism. Communities witnessing escalating droughts, floods, warnings,intense debate,and compromised outcomes—erodes public trust in the entire process. The language of diplomats, filled with "notes of concern" and "calls to action," begins to ring hollow against the tangible reality of a changing climate, measured in lost homes and compromised harvests. Ultimately, the summits' legacy is one of painful contradiction. They have established an essential global framework and a shared scientific baseline,proving that dialogue persists even amidst disagreement.However,theyhave alsodemonstrated the severelimitations ofvoluntarycooperationin theface of a crisis demanding immediate,transformative sacrifice.The continued failure tobend the emissions curve suggests these conferences are necessary, but woefully insufficient, highlighting a profound disconnectbetweenourcollectiveknowledge and ourcollectivewill.

questions.passage_text: THI ONLINE -CHIEN THUAT XU'LY CAUHOI PARAPHRASING(BUOI3) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus 2: Chinh phuc cau hoi 10 diem Co Vu Thi Mai Phro'ng Reading Passage 1 The character of winter is undergoing a profound transformation across many regions. The iconic imagery of relentless frost, deep snowdrifts,and frozen landscapes is increasingly replaced by a persistent, unsettling mildness. This shift transcends mere seasonal variation, representing a tangible symptom of our altered climate.Winters are demonstrably warming at a rate far exceeding other seasons,a trend that disruptslong-established naturalrhythms andsocietalexpectations. The ecological consequences of this warming are extensive and deeply concerning. Many plant and animal speciesrely onprolongedcold periodsfor dormancy,reproduction,and pest control.Warmer temperatures disrupt these critical cycles, leading to premature bud bursts vulnerable to late frosts and allowing invasive species and pathogens tothrive.Furthermore,diminished snowpack acts as a crucial water reservoir;its reductionthreatens summerwatersecurityfor countless communities and ecosystems, creating a cascade of environmental stress. From a human perspective,the impacts are equally complex and significant.While reduced heating costs and fewer extreme cold events offer superficial benefits,the negative ramifications are severe. Economies dependent on reliable winter tourism, from sking to ice festivals, face existential threats. Agriculture suffers as crops lose their protective winter chill, and unpredictable weather patterns destabilise growing seasons.Perhaps most critically, the loss of cultural touchstones and traditional winter activities represents an intangible erosion of heritage and communal joy. Ultimately, these anomalously warm winters serve as an unequivocal and sobering reminder of anthropogenic climate change.They are not a gentle reprieve but a clear signal of systemic environmental imbalance.Addressing this challengerequires a global commitment toreducinggreenhouse gas emissions and implementing robust adaptation strategies. The future of winter itself, in all its vital ecological and cultural roles,depends on the actions we take today. Question1.Which of the following isNOT mentioned as a scene of a winter? A. heavy winter thunderstorms B. constant, biting frost C.scenes of land locked in ice D.thickmounds ofwinter snow

questions.passage_text: THI ONLINE -CHIEN THUAT XU'LY CAUHOI PARAPHRASING(BUOI3) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus 2: Chinh phuc cau hoi 10 diem Co Vu Thi Mai Phro'ng Reading Passage 1 The character of winter is undergoing a profound transformation across many regions. The iconic imagery of relentless frost, deep snowdrifts,and frozen landscapes is increasingly replaced by a persistent, unsettling mildness. This shift transcends mere seasonal variation, representing a tangible symptom of our altered climate.Winters are demonstrably warming at a rate far exceeding other seasons,a trend that disruptslong-established naturalrhythms andsocietalexpectations. The ecological consequences of this warming are extensive and deeply concerning. Many plant and animal speciesrely onprolongedcold periodsfor dormancy,reproduction,and pest control.Warmer temperatures disrupt these critical cycles, leading to premature bud bursts vulnerable to late frosts and allowing invasive species and pathogens tothrive.Furthermore,diminished snowpack acts as a crucial water reservoir;its reductionthreatens summerwatersecurityfor countless communities and ecosystems, creating a cascade of environmental stress. From a human perspective,the impacts are equally complex and significant.While reduced heating costs and fewer extreme cold events offer superficial benefits,the negative ramifications are severe. Economies dependent on reliable winter tourism, from sking to ice festivals, face existential threats. Agriculture suffers as crops lose their protective winter chill, and unpredictable weather patterns destabilise growing seasons.Perhaps most critically, the loss of cultural touchstones and traditional winter activities represents an intangible erosion of heritage and communal joy. Ultimately, these anomalously warm winters serve as an unequivocal and sobering reminder of anthropogenic climate change.They are not a gentle reprieve but a clear signal of systemic environmental imbalance.Addressing this challengerequires a global commitment toreducinggreenhouse gas emissions and implementing robust adaptation strategies. The future of winter itself, in all its vital ecological and cultural roles,depends on the actions we take today. Question1.Which of the following isNOT mentioned as a scene of a winter? A. heavy winter thunderstorms B. constant, biting frost C.scenes of land locked in ice D.thickmounds ofwinter snow

questions.question_text: The phrase take into account in paragraph 2 is closest in meaning to B.access C. utilise D. establish A.consider DangKyKhoa H cOnlineT iFanpage: Tai Li u.KhoaH.cUniMap Viauvenloichinh
questions.passage_text: TAILIEUDOCQUYEN DIKEMKHOAHOC LUYENTAPCHUYENSAUDQCHIEU10CAU(BUOI1) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus 2: Luyen doc dien va doc hieu chuyen sau Co Vi Thi Mai Phuong ReadthefollowingpassageaboutgreencitiesandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answer to each of thequestions from 1 to10. When people hear the word “city", they usually imagine concrete, neon signs, and lots of air pollution. [1]Thanks to some clever and concerned architects,thisimage is starting to change.[Il]The concept of green cities aims to bring some beauty from rural environments into urban areas. [lll] Green cities are also trying to improve the efficiency of cities and raise the standard of living for people who stay in them. [IV] The term“green cities"refers to cities where builders take into account several environmental factors before that city is constructed or redesigned. For starters, green cities are designed to be more physically appealing than traditional cities from the past. In addition,the mobility of citizens should be considered,so public transportation can be more convenient and travel time to work canbe reduced. Energy sources should also be evaluated with the goal of minimising air pollution. Whenit comes togoinggreen,thecity ofBarcelonais faraheadofitstime.Since1859,its designershaveconsciouslytried tocreatesustainableurbandesigns.Thenin1992,thecityreceived a well-deserved makeover before hosting the Olympic Games.In recent years,Barcelona has upgraded its transportation system.This has reduced air pollution as more and more workers are using public transportation instead of their own vehicles. Barcelona also boasts one of the best recycling programmes in the world.Visitors will find clear,colour-coded trash bins throughout the city where they can put all of their materials. It is no secret in Portland, Oregon is one of the greenest cities in the US. Since 1903,it has been an example of what American cities can accomplish with careful planning. Instead of filling its area with office buildings, Portland has plenty of green space for activities. There are 119 kilometres of running, hiking, and bike trails that allow citizens to experience the great outdoors. The city also offers residents creative energy plans that give them the opportunity to pay a bit extra for clean energy sources.For about $3to$9extraeachmonth,homeowners canchoosesolarorwind power and helptheenvironment. These are only a small sampling of the green cities around the world. As more people experience the pleasure of visiting green cities, it seems certain that the trend of urban areas going green will continue far into the future. (Adapted fromEnglish 12) Question1.Wherein paragraph1 does thefollowing sentence best fit? It is not only about looking better though. D.[IV] A.[1] B. [1] C. [] Question2.Theword theminparagraph1refersto D. urban areas A.rural environments B.green cities C. people

Whenit comes togoinggreen,thecity ofBarcelonais faraheadofitstime.Since1859,its designershaveconsciouslytried tocreatesustainableurbandesigns.Thenin1992,thecityreceived a well-deserved makeover before hosting the Olympic Games.In recent years,Barcelona has upgraded its transportation system.This has reduced air pollution as more and more workers are using public transportation instead of their own vehicles. Barcelona also boasts one of the best recycling programmes in the world.Visitors will find clear,colour-coded trash bins throughout the city where they can put all of their materials. It is no secret in Portland, Oregon is one of the greenest cities in the US. Since 1903,it has been an example of what American cities can accomplish with careful planning. For starters, green cities are designed to be more physically appealing than traditional cities from the past. In addition,the mobility of citizens should be considered,so public transportation can be more convenient and travel time to work canbe reduced. Energy sources should also be evaluated with the goal of minimising air pollution. Whenit comes togoinggreen,thecity ofBarcelonais faraheadofitstime.Since1859,its designershaveconsciouslytried tocreatesustainableurbandesigns.Thenin1992,thecityreceived a well-deserved makeover before hosting the Olympic Games.In recent years,Barcelona has upgraded its transportation system.This has reduced air pollution as more and more workers are using public transportation instead of their own vehicles. Barcelona also boasts one of the best recycling programmes in the world.Visitors will find clear,colour-coded trash bins throughout the city where they can put all of their materials. It is no secret in Portland, Oregon is one of the greenest cities in the US. Since 1903,it has been an example of what American cities can accomplish with careful planning. Instead of filling its area with office buildings, Portland has plenty of green space for activities. There are 119 kilometres of running, hiking, and bike trails that allow citizens to experience the great outdoors. The city also offers residents creative energy plans that give them the opportunity to pay a bit extra for clean energy sources.For about $3to$9extraeachmonth,homeowners canchoosesolarorwind power and helptheenvironment. These are only a small sampling of the green cities around the world. As more people experience the pleasure of visiting green cities, it seems certain that the trend of urban areas going green will continue far into the future. (Adapted fromEnglish 12) Question1.Wherein paragraph1 does thefollowing sentence best fit? It is not only about looking better though. D.[IV] A.[1] B. [1] C. questions.passage_text: PO TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHOC LUYEN TAP CHUYEN SAU DQC HIEU 10 CAU (BUOI 2) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Luyendocdienvadochieuchuyensau Co Vi Thi Mai Phuong Read thefollowingpassage aboutgreenspacesinurban areasandmark theletterA,B,C,orD to indicatethecorrectanswer toeachofthequestionsfrom1to10. [1] Cities are home to skyscrapers and apartment buildings,and it's rare to find wide, open spaces challenging to incorporate greenery into neighbourhoods. [lll] It's a popular idea, and now rooftop gardens and green walls havebeen springingupin cities around theworld.[IV] There are many benefits to having green spaces in the urban landscape. Adding gardens to rooftops or walls can create a pleasant environment - what was once a grey cement wall can become a colourful, blooming garden. The CaxiaForum art gallery in Madrid, Spain, is a famous example - one of its walls is covered with15,000 plants from over 250 different species.In other cities,greenwalls arebeing used more functionally, to cover up construction sites and empty buildings and to prominently decorate the lobbies of office buildings. Usingplantsto cover walls and rooftops can alsokeep cities cooler in the summer.Buildings and roads absorb the sun's heat and hold it, causing a building or neighbourhood to stay warmer longer. Plants, on the other hand,provide anenormous amount of shade.There is evidence thatgrowing a roof orwall gardencanlower abuilding'senergycosts.Manycitiesoffer taxdiscounts tobusinesseswith these features. In NewYork City, public schools plant rooftop gardens that can reduce heating and cooling costs. In addition to saving the school money,teachers and parents love the gardens because of their educational value - it's a fun and healthy way for their kids to investigate the world around them.“For the children, it's exciting when you grow something edible,” said Lauren Fontana, principal of a New York public school. These green spaces are also used to grow food. In recent years, rooftop gardens have slowly been included in the “local food movement". This is based on the concept that locally grown food reduces pollution since it does not have to be transported far. Vegetables are being grown in rooftop gardens by schools,churches,neighbourhoods and even restaurants.ChefRickBayless serves“RooftopSalsa”at his restaurant in Chicago, USA, using only ingredients grown in his rooftop garden. Rooftop gardens and green walls may require a bit more effort to grow and maintain. However, hard work always brings rewards,andwithgreen spaces,therewards areplentiful (AdaptedfromEnglish12) Question1.Where in paragraph1 does thefollowing sentencebest fit? One creativesolutionis togrowplants in unused areas likewalls orrooftops. D.[IV] B.[1] A.[1] C. [1!]

raw_extract.block:

areas into pleasant environments, such as the CaixaForum art gallery in Madrid.

CaixaForum art gallery, which features thousands of plants.
C.Green spaces improve urban areas,with walls and rooftops covered in plants used decoratively and
functionally, like at the CaixaForum art gallery, construction sites and office lobbies.
D. The CaxiaForum art gallery in Madrid is a prime example of a green wall, showing how plants can
enhance buildings and create beautiful urban spaces.

questions.relative_path: MP/TỪ VỰNG CHUYÊN SÂU/CHUYÊN SÂU ĐỌC HIỂU 10 CÂU/plus2_buoi3.pdf
questions.question_text: What is indicated about Facebook in paragraph 1? A.It has been the most popular form of social media of all time. B. Its popularity has declined significantly in recent years. C. It became popular before Twitter did. D.It is the only site that allows instant communication

options.B: Its popularity has declined significantly in recent years.
options.C: It became popular before Twitter did.

questions.passage_text: TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHOC LUYEN TAP CHUYEN SAU DQC HIEU 10 CAU (BUOI 3) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Luyendocdienvadochieuchuyensau Co Vi Thi Mai Phuong ReadthefollowingpassageaboutTwitterandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answertoeachofthequestionsfrom1to10. At the start of the social media revolution, Facebook quickly became the most popular site for sharing information with others. [l] However, it is not the only service that people can use to express themselves. [l] Those who believe in the old saying that “more is less" may want to check out Twitter. [lll] Part of Twitter's appeal is that it limits users to 140 characters whenever they update their status. [IV] Twitter is a microblogging service that was created by four American programmers in 2006. The founders chose the name Twitter because the word has two definitions:“a short burst ofinformation”and "the sound that little birds make", both of which fit the description of what their service provides. Twitter is not just about words though. Users can also share videos and pictures.Another thing which truly sets Twitter apart from its competitors is the hashtag.Hashtags are a special way of labelling and classifying information. They help people interested in a subject to find related posts and content. Twitter has also allowed people to easily connect with others who they would have never had access tointhepast.ThishasenabledfanstointeractwiththeirfavouritecelebritiesbyfollowingtheirTwitter updates and sendingthemdirectmessages.Of course,thereis noguarantee that thefamouspersonwill respond, as celebrities may receive thousands of messages each day. However, there have been some pretty stunning interactions. In 2011, singer Justin Timberlake made headlines when he accepted an invitation from a female soldier to attend a Marine Corps ball.In return,many celebrities find it gratifying to know some of their supporters more closely. However, there is a dark side to Twitter conversations. Some people don't think before sending out tweets that can cause outrage by making inappropriate or insensitive remarks. (AdaptedfromEnglish12)

[IV] Twitter is a microblogging service that was created by four American programmers in 2006. The founders chose the name Twitter because the word has two definitions:“a short burst ofinformation”and "the sound that little birds make", both of which fit the description of what their service provides. Twitter is not just about words though. Users can also share videos and pictures.Another thing which truly sets Twitter apart from its competitors is the hashtag.Hashtags are a special way of labelling and classifying information. They help people interested in a subject to find related posts and content. Twitter has also allowed people to easily connect with others who they would have never had access tointhepast.ThishasenabledfanstointeractwiththeirfavouritecelebritiesbyfollowingtheirTwitter updates and sendingthemdirectmessages.Of course,thereis noguarantee that thefamouspersonwill respond, as celebrities may receive thousands of messages each day. However, there have been some pretty stunning interactions. In 2011, singer Justin Timberlake made headlines when he accepted an invitation from a female soldier to attend a Marine Corps ball.In return,many celebrities find it gratifying to know some of their supporters more closely. However, there is a dark side to Twitter conversations. Some people don't think before sending out tweets that can cause outrage by making inappropriate or insensitive remarks. (AdaptedfromEnglish12)

questions.question_text: Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence in paragraph 1? AlthoughtheUKisanurbansociety,moreandmorepeoplearechoosingtoliveontheedgeof urbanareaswithmanyrelocatingtothecountryside

options.A: While the UK is predominantly urban, a growing number of individuals are opting to settle on the
options.B: Despite being an urbanised society, the UK is seeing fewer people choosing rural areas and more
options.D: Although the UK is largely rural, there is a notable trend of people leaving urban centres to reside in

questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co vu Thi Mai PhShared By Faypage:dTyiLit taKhga hnguniMap TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHOC LUYENTAPCHUYENSAUDOCHIEU10CAU (BUOI4) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Luyendocdienva dochieuchuyensau Co Vi Thi Mai Phuong Read thefollowingpassageabouturbanisationintheUKandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicate thecorrect answer toeach of thequestions from 1 to10. As a country industrialises, the number of people living in urban areas tends to increase. The UK urbanised during the18th and19thcenturies.Peoplemigratedfromrural areas tourban areaswherethere was employment in the newfactories.The area of cities known as the inner city developed during this time as rows of terraced housing were built for workers. Today the UK is a mostly urban society,with 90 per cent of the population living in towns or cities. Although the UK is an urban society, more and more people are choosing to live on the edge of urban areas with many relocating to the countryside. This is called counter-urbanisation. As more people move to the edge of towns and cities, traffic congestion may get worse. Many people will drive their cars into the city centre to get to work. It is compounded by people being brought into the city on large roads or motorways. These roads then link up with smaller, older, narrower roads in the city centre. This causes a bottleneck and congestion. Inequalities exist in all urban areas. [ll Inequality means extreme differences between poverty and wealth, as well as in people's well-being and access to things like jobs, housing and education. [ll] Inequalitiesmay occur inhousingprovision, access toservices,accesstoopenland,and safety and security. [lll] This is because the inner-city is typically a zone with older housing and declining industry. [IV] Governments and planners often step in to help redevelop run-down inner-city areas. Inner-city redevelopments, such as those in London's Docklands or Manchester's Salford Quays, may improve the physical environment of the area and improve the quality of housing. The old industrial jobs are often replaced by skilled jobs and new people move to the area. (Adapted from English 12)

questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co vu Thi Mai PhShared By Faypage:dTyiLit taKhga hnguniMap TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHOC LUYENTAPCHUYENSAUDOCHIEU10CAU (BUOI4) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Luyendocdienva dochieuchuyensau Co Vi Thi Mai Phuong Read thefollowingpassageabouturbanisationintheUKandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicate thecorrect answer toeach of thequestions from 1 to10. As a country industrialises, the number of people living in urban areas tends to increase. The UK urbanised during the18th and19thcenturies.Peoplemigratedfromrural areas tourban areaswherethere was employment in the newfactories.The area of cities known as the inner city developed during this time as rows of terraced housing were built for workers. Today the UK is a mostly urban society,with 90 per cent of the population living in towns or cities. Although the UK is an urban society, more and more people are choosing to live on the edge of urban areas with many relocating to the countryside. This is called counter-urbanisation. As more people move to the edge of towns and cities, traffic congestion may get worse. Many people will drive their cars into the city centre to get to work. It is compounded by people being brought into the city on large roads or motorways. These roads then link up with smaller, older, narrower roads in the city centre. This causes a bottleneck and congestion. Inequalities exist in all urban areas. [ll Inequality means extreme differences between poverty and wealth, as well as in people's well-being and access to things like jobs, housing and education. [ll] Inequalitiesmay occur inhousingprovision, access toservices,accesstoopenland,and safety and security. [lll] This is because the inner-city is typically a zone with older housing and declining industry. [IV] Governments and planners often step in to help redevelop run-down inner-city areas. Inner-city redevelopments, such as those in London's Docklands or Manchester's Salford Quays, may improve the physical environment of the area and improve the quality of housing. The old industrial jobs are often replaced by skilled jobs and new people move to the area. (Adapted from English 12)

D.The government should take more proactive measures to preserve deep-rooted traditions.

questions.question_text: The word it in paragraph 2 refers to D.business C. philosophy B. desire A.situation
questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co Vu Thi Mai PhuhayedBy FaypageuTyiLat uaKhgahnguUnilMap TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHOC LUYEN TAP CHUYEN SAU DQCHIEU 10 CAU (BUOI5) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Luyendocdienvadochieuchuyensau Co Vu Thi Mai Phuong ReadthefollowingpassageaboutChrisMoon andmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answertoeachofthequestionsfrom1to10. It would be easy to feel intimidated by someone who has survived abduction by theKhmer Rouge, run theLondonmarathonshortly afterlosingtheirlowerright arm andleginanaccident and appeared on the BBC's Parkinson show.Yet Chris Moon, 39,is refreshingly down to earth, even to the point of finding it difficult to keep the initial appointments for this interview, proving that even superheroes can't be in two places at once. At present,Chris runs a company called Making theBest(MTB)tohelp clients solve problems in human resources and security management. You don't need to speak to him for long before you realise that this is a particularly appropriate title. Not only does he talk with an almost evangelical zeal about his desire to empower others to get the best out of themselves and their business,but his own philosophy is all about making thebest of any situation,howevergrimit mayseem. "[l] I decided it was about doing the best we can, reaching our full potential and making use of our talents. [Il] I wanted to make a difference, do something I could be trained in but also grow as a person, MilitaryPoliceand alsoservedwithseveralinfantryunits.[IV] After leaving the army, he felt he wanted to do some sort of charity work, and when he heard that the British charity, the HALO Trust - which specialises in mine clearance - wanted an army officer with agricultural andcommandexperience,hewaskeen togetinvolved.Itwaswhileclearing mines in Cambodia that he was abducted by the Khmer Rouge, an experience which, not surprisingly, had a profound effect on him. "When we were first kidnapped, I thought I had no control as I kept thinking I can't do anything about this.But my one thought was never to assume the role of victim.It would have been so easy to give up,but I quickly realised that fear is a state of inactivity. You have to be positive and pursue logic and reason. That was, and still is, my driving force." Thethingshelearnedfrom thisexperiencehelpedhimtosurvivewhenhewasblownupby amine two years later, while walking in a supposedly clear area in Mozambique. He lost his lower right arm and leg, but does not consider himself a victim.He accepts responsibility because he chose to work in mined areas. So, after all the experiences he has been through, is he surprised to find himself running his own business?“My mum’s cousin had an extremely successful dairy business and when I was very young, he said to me:‘When you've done all the other things you want to do,set up a business and help the best out ofpeople.Heobviouslyhad it right." (Adapted from Upstream) Question1.Thephrasedowntoearthinparagraph1mostlymeans I respectful A.affordable B.unexpected C. practical Dang Ky Khoa H c Online T iFanpage: Tai Li u Khoa Hc SUniMap chongudikhac! Viguyenloichinhdang Bien soan: Co Va Thi Mai PhShared By Faypage uTai Lit uaK hga hnguunilMap

questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co Vu Thi Mai PhuhayedBy FaypageuTyiLat uaKhgahnguUnilMap TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHOC LUYEN TAP CHUYEN SAU DQCHIEU 10 CAU (BUOI5) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Luyendocdienvadochieuchuyensau Co Vu Thi Mai Phuong ReadthefollowingpassageaboutChrisMoon andmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answertoeachofthequestionsfrom1to10. It would be easy to feel intimidated by someone who has survived abduction by theKhmer Rouge, run theLondonmarathonshortly afterlosingtheirlowerright arm andleginanaccident and appeared on the BBC's Parkinson show.Yet Chris Moon, 39,is refreshingly down to earth, even to the point of finding it difficult to keep the initial appointments for this interview, proving that even superheroes can't be in two places at once. At present,Chris runs a company called Making theBest(MTB)tohelp clients solve problems in human resources and security management. You don't need to speak to him for long before you realise that this is a particularly appropriate title. Not only does he talk with an almost evangelical zeal about his desire to empower others to get the best out of themselves and their business,but his own philosophy is all about making thebest of any situation,howevergrimit mayseem. "[l] I decided it was about doing the best we can, reaching our full potential and making use of our talents. [Il] I wanted to make a difference, do something I could be trained in but also grow as a person, MilitaryPoliceand alsoservedwithseveralinfantryunits.[IV] After leaving the army, he felt he wanted to do some sort of charity work, and when he heard that the British charity, the HALO Trust - which specialises in mine clearance - wanted an army officer with agricultural andcommandexperience,hewaskeen togetinvolved.Itwaswhileclearing mines in Cambodia that he was abducted by the Khmer Rouge, an experience which, not surprisingly, had a profound effect on him. "When we were first kidnapped, I thought I had no control as I kept thinking I can't do anything about this.But my one thought was never to assume the role of victim.It would have been so easy to give up,but I quickly realised that fear is a state of inactivity. You have to be positive and pursue logic and reason. That was, and still is, my driving force." Thethingshelearnedfrom thisexperiencehelpedhimtosurvivewhenhewasblownupby amine two years later, while walking in a supposedly clear area in Mozambique. He lost his lower right arm and leg, but does not consider himself a victim.He accepts responsibility because he chose to work in mined areas. So, after all the experiences he has been through, is he surprised to find himself running his own business?“My mum’s cousin had an extremely successful dairy business and when I was very young, he said to me:‘When you've done all the other things you want to do,set up a business and help the best out ofpeople.Heobviouslyhad it right." (Adapted from Upstream) Question1.Thephrasedowntoearthinparagraph1mostlymeans I respectful A.affordable B.unexpected C. practical Dang Ky Khoa H c Online T iFanpage: Tai Li u Khoa Hc SUniMap chongudikhac! questions.question_text: engaging Readthefollowingpassageabout jobopportunitiesin theminingindustry andmarktheletterA,B,C, orD toindicate thecorrect answer to each of thequestions from 6to13. With a multitude of vocational choices available for school leavers,the mining industry offers a heavy machinery operators,there are many opportunities for school leavers tofind a career in mining that suits their interests and abilities. Playing a vital role in the global economy and society,the mining industry is a great place for school leavers to learn new skills and gain valuable work experience.As mining companies offer a variety of training and development assistance programmes to those in their employ, schoolleavers can start their careers with a solid foundation and build on their skills over time. Contrarytopopularbelief,theminingindustryisnotsolelylimited toengineeringrolesand manual labour.It offers a diverse array of career paths that cater tovarious interests and skill sets.For example, geologists and geoscientists play a critical role in identifying and exploring mineral resources. They use their expertise to assess theviability of mining projects and ensure sustainable practices. toindicatethecorrectanswertoeachofthequestionsfrom14to21.
When you leave secondary school, you have different options,including going to a university or a
vocational school, getting a job or taking a gap year. Sometimes choices may make you confused, so you
should takeinto account all your options before making any plans for the future.Below are some things
you may like to trybeforemakingyour decision.
If there are teachers you really love or look up to,you can ask them for some advice.Your teachers
have been observing you for months, so they know your strengths,talents,and personality.You may
shareyour dreams and concernswith them and askthem togiveyou anhonest opinion about what option
may be suitable for you.

options.A: His thoughts on cyberbullying has been exaggerated.
options.C: Cyberbullying is more serious than people's initial thoughts.

questions.passage_text: Read the following passage about an opinion on cyberbullying and mark the letter A,B,C,or D to indicatethecorrect answertoeachof thequestionsfrom6to13. Cyberbullying hasbeen exaggerated according toresearch byDr Olweus of theUniversity of Bergen. His survey reports that 18% of students have been bullied but only 5% of bullying occurs online. It also tells us that face-to-face bullying is still the way most people are bullied. Surprisingly, cyberbullying has all the newspaper headlines, giving the impression of a massive problem that is damaging our children's mental health. I'mnotsaying thatcyberbullyingdoesnothavenegative consequences.Targetsof cyberbullying,like thetargets offace-to-facebullying,sufferfrom anxiety,depression andself-harm. For me,cyberbullying is simply a new addition to an old problem, so our schools can deal with cyberbullying within their existing anti-bullying strategies.I think parents alsohave a responsibility for teaching their children toignore nasty messages and not be oversensitive. Young people also need the confidence to use the block or report button if they receive abusive communication online.Hopefully,by learning how to neutralise an online bully,they will learn valuable life skills for managing any unpleasant people they meet in their future studies and work. (AdaptedfromC21Smart) Vi quyen lgi chinh dang cua chinh cac em, TUYET DOI KHONG chia se tai lieu DOc QUYEN nay cho nguroi khac! Dang Ky Khoa H c Online T iFanpage: Tai Li u Khoa H c UniMap

Surprisingly, cyberbullying has all the newspaper headlines, giving the impression of a massive problem that is damaging our children's mental health. questions.question_text: .grants A.where whose D.whom B,that Question5. ReadthefollowingpassageaboutdifficultjobsandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answertoeachofthequestionsfrom6to14. Jeff Haslam spends much of his time working underground in London's sewers.These tunnels are crucial for transporting wastewater and other refuse away from the city. Jeff's main job is to keep these clean."We work in teams of six,"he explains,noting the dirty and dark conditions of their work. He wears specialised clothing, a mask, and a helmet with a light to navigate these challenging environments. In contrast, Charlie Radley works as a fisherman in the cold waters of Alaska. Each fishing trip lasts about a week, and the weather can be extremely harsh.“It's often stormy and very cold,” Charlie says,highlighting the need for warm,waterproof clothing. While he has the potential to earn a good income,it greatly depends on the success of their catch. If they don't bring in enough fish, his earnings significantly drop. SandySmith is a lumberjack who enjoysworking outdoors.She noticed a job advertisement for lumberjacks online and decided to apply, despite the long hours and modest pay. "It's a very dangerous job," Sandy admits,emphasising the risks associated with using heavy machinery in remote areas. She is always cautious, as medicalfacilities are always far away. Despitetheirdifferentoccupations,Jeff,Charlie,andSandyallfacesignificantchallengesin theirjobs.Each onedealswithuniquedifficulties,frommaintaining sewersandfishinginharsh conditions to cutting down trees.Their storiesillustrate the dedication required for roles that are essential to society,even if they often go unrecognised. (AdaptedfromSolutions) Vi quyen lqi chinh dang cua chinh cac em. TUYET DOl KHONG chia se tai lieu DOc QuYEN nay cho nguoi khac! Dang Ky Khoa H c Online T i Fanpage: Tai Li u Khoa H c UniMap

questions.question_text: B.respect Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B, C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of the questions from 6 to 13. Zoos are hugely popular attractions for adults and children alike.But are they actually a good thing? Critics of zoos would argue that animals often suffer physically and mentally by being enclosed. Even the best artificial environments can't come close to matching the space, diversity,and freedom that animals have in their natural habitats.This deprivation causes many zoo animals to become stressed or mentally ill. On the other hand, by bringing people and animals together, zoos have the potential to educate the public about conservation issues and inspire people to protect animals and their habitats. One of the most important modern functions of zoos is supporting international breeding programmes, particularly for endangered species. In the wild, some of the rarest species have difficulty in finding mates and breeding, and they might also be threatened by poachers, loss of their habitat and predators.A good zoowill enable these species tolive andbreed in a secure environment.However, opponentsofzoossaythatthevastmajorityofcaptivebreedingprogrammesdonotreleaseanimals back into the wild. Last summer,I travelled toEcuador tovolunteer at a rescue centre.I hadheard about the shocking
tradeinwild animalsanddecidedIhadtodosomething.
Everyyear a large number of animals are caught in theAmazon Rainforest in South America and
then sold as pets or killed for theirfur,skin or meat.This is usually against the law,and the animals are
often kept in verybad conditions while they are transported. Sometimes these animals are rescued and
end upinacentrelikemine,wherethey arecaredforproperly
Our daily life followed a routine. Every morning at 7.30 we fed the animals and cleaned their
cages.There were many different animals at the centre, including monkeys, parrots, snakes and various
small creatures.Later,we worked on projects such as tidyingup the centre,making repairs and building
new areasfor the animals.On some daysIalsogave tours tovisitors.In the afternoon,wefed the animals

rejected_questions.passage_text: Read the following passage and mark the letter A, B,C, or D to indicate the correct answer to each of thequestionsfrom1to8. The rise of the gig economy has transformed how millions of people think about work, stability, and personal choice. As traditional full-time jobs become harder to secure in many cities, more individuals areturning toshort-term,flexible opportunities toearn anincome.This shift affects not onlyworkers but also companies and communities that must adapt to new expectations about work hours,responsibility, andlong-termcommitment. For example, Linh, a 27-year-old graphic designer in Ho Chi Minh City, once worked in a small advertising agency. After experiencing constant overtime and limited career growth, she left to become a freelance designer. Today, she accepts projects through online platforms, from creating brand logos to designing social media content for international clients.Linh enjoys thefreedom to select the jobs that interest her, organise her schedule, and negotiate her own rates. However, she also admits that the income can be unpredictable,and she must manage everything—from marketing to customer service—on her own. The gig economy offers significant advantages.Workers like Linh can develop a broad skill set, build global networks, and enjoy a sense of autonomy rarely found in traditional roles. Companies, meanwhile,benefit from accessing skilled workers without long-term contracts,allowing them to respond quicklytomarketchanges.Manygovernmentshavealsorecognisedthegigeconomy'spotential to boostinnovationandcreateopportunitiesforyoungpeople. Still, the model presents serious challenges. Gig workers often lack health insurance, paid leave, and legalprotection,leavingthemvulnerableduringillnessoreconomicdownturns.Competition ondigital platforms can also be intense, pushing prices down and reducing job security.As the gig economy continues to expand, policymakers must decide how to balance flexibility with fairness, ensuring that people like Linh can pursue meaningful work without sacrificing financial stability or prospects.

rejected_questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co Vu Thi Mai Phghaged By Funpage:drairhfttakHoanganhaiNiphuong.vn TAILIEUDOCQUYEN DIKEMKHOAHOC TU'DUY TUYEN TINH (LINEARTHINKING) Docquyenva duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Chinhphuccauhoi10diem CoVu Thi Mai Phuo'ng Read thefollowingpassageandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrectanswertoeachof thequestionsfrom1to8. StephenWiltshire is aBritish artist admired for his astonishing ability to draw detailed cityscapes entirelyfrom memory. He was born in London in 1974 toWest Indian parents and diagnosed with autism at the age of three.As a young child, he hardly spoke and often seemed withdrawn from the people around him. Yet, while words were difficult for him, he found comfort in sketching cars, animals, and buildings.His teachers quickly noticed that his pictures contained remarkable detail and perspective. When Stephen was eight, he received his first major commission to draw Salisbury Cathedral, and that opportunity introduced him to the public.Later,at the City and Guilds of London Art School,he developed his technique and gained a deeper understanding of architecture.His talent is extraordinary: after observing a city once, often from a helicopter, he can reproduce its skyline with incredible accuracy. His panoramic drawings of NewYork,Tokyo, and Rome capture not only every building but also the rhythm ofurbanlife,as if the citiesbreathe through hispencil. Today, Stephen's works are exhibited internationally, and he manages his own gallery in London's Royal Opera Arcade. His art has been purchased by collectors, featured in documentaries, and praised by critics who call him“the human camera.”In 2006,he was awarded the MBE for his services to art. StephenWiltshire continuestotravel,observe,and draw.Hesays that art allowshimto connect deeply with people and places.Each new work reflects his sharp memory, patient technique, and unique way of seeing the world — transforming everyday landscapes into vivid expressions of life and emotion. rejected_questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT cited in paragraph 2 as quietly influencing teens’ sense of achievement and appearance? A.idealised lifestyles C. curated images editedvideos B.self-criticism
rejected_questions.passage_text: Adolescence is widely recognised as one of the most transformative and demanding stages of life. Teenagers must navigate rapid physical and emotional changes while trying tomeet academic expectations and maintain social connections. This period often brings a mixture of excitement and uncertainty, as young people attempt to understand themselves and their place in an increasingly complex world. As a result, many teens experience internal pressure that is difficult to communicate openly. One of the most prominent challenges is the influence of digital culture. Social media platforms allow teenagers to express creativity and stay connected, but they also expose them to constant comparison. Curated images, edited videos, and idealised lifestyles can quietly affect how teens evaluate their own achievements and appearance. The fear of missing out, along with the desire for online validation, can intensify feelings of anxiety or self-criticism. Over time, these pressures may shape how young people perceive success,happiness, and personal worth. Another significantissueinvolvesthestruggletoformastableidentity.Teenagersmustmake decisions about future careers, relationships, and personal values at a time when their minds are still developing. Conflicts may arisewhen their interests_do not align with the expectations of parents, teachers,or peers.This tension can lead to frustration or confusion as teens attempt to define who they about the future, especially in fast-changing societies. Supporting teenagers requires consistentguidance and empatheticcommunication.Schools can play a vital role by promoting emotional resilience and teaching practical coping strategies. Families, meanwhile, can help by listening without judgement and encouraging healthy routines. When adults acknowledge the complexity of teen issues and offer reliable support, young people gain the confidence and stability they need to navigate this pivotal stage and move towards adulthood with greater clarity and strength.

rejected_questions.passage_text: Adolescence is widely recognised as one of the most transformative and demanding stages of life. Teenagers must navigate rapid physical and emotional changes while trying tomeet academic expectations and maintain social connections. This period often brings a mixture of excitement and uncertainty, as young people attempt to understand themselves and their place in an increasingly complex world. As a result, many teens experience internal pressure that is difficult to communicate openly. One of the most prominent challenges is the influence of digital culture. Social media platforms allow teenagers to express creativity and stay connected, but they also expose them to constant comparison. Curated images, edited videos, and idealised lifestyles can quietly affect how teens evaluate their own achievements and appearance. The fear of missing out, along with the desire for online validation, can intensify feelings of anxiety or self-criticism. Over time, these pressures may shape how young people perceive success,happiness, and personal worth. Another significantissueinvolvesthestruggletoformastableidentity.Teenagersmustmake decisions about future careers, relationships, and personal values at a time when their minds are still developing. Conflicts may arisewhen their interests_do not align with the expectations of parents, teachers,or peers.This tension can lead to frustration or confusion as teens attempt to define who they about the future, especially in fast-changing societies. Supporting teenagers requires consistentguidance and empatheticcommunication.Schools can play a vital role by promoting emotional resilience and teaching practical coping strategies. Families, meanwhile, can help by listening without judgement and encouraging healthy routines. When adults acknowledge the complexity of teen issues and offer reliable support, young people gain the confidence and stability they need to navigate this pivotal stage and move towards adulthood with greater clarity and strength.

rejected_questions.question_text: Which of the following locations is NOT mentioned as a site for the new form of farming? A.suburbanbackyards B.industrial zones D. unused rooftops C.abandoned urbanlots
rejected_questions.passage_text: Bien soan: Co Vu Thi Mai PhShatedEBy Fanpage:dTairbitutaK hrenbaolcoiMighuong.vn TAILIEUDOCQUYENDIKEMKHOAHQC TU'DUYTUYENTINH(LINEARTHINKING) LUYENTAPCHUYENSAU TU'DUY TUYENTINH(BUOI4) Doc quyen va duy nhat tai khoa hoc Plus2:Chinhphuccauhoi10diem Co Vu Thi Mai Phuong Read thefollowingpassageandmarktheletterA,B,C,orDtoindicatethecorrect answertoeachof the questions from 1 to8. in the most unexpected of places.Abandoned urban lots,industrial zones,and even rooftops are now host to a new form of farming. This innovative approach utilises repurposed shipping containers, transforming these steel boxes into highly controlled environments for growing food. This is container farming, a methodthat challenges our traditionalperceptionsof agriculture andbrings thefarm directlytothe consumer. The interior of a container farm is a world away from an open field. Every aspect of the environment is meticulously managed. Instead of soil, plants grow vertically in nutrient-rich water solutions or mist, a technique known as hydroponics or aeroponics. LED lights, tuned to specific wavelengths, replace the sun,providing a consistent and optimal light source for photosynthesis. Advanced climate control systems maintain the perfect temperature and humidity, creating an eternal summer inside the steel walls, completelyindependentoftheweatheroutside. This controlled environment offers significant advantages. Container farms can produce yields vastlyhigher than traditional farming on the same footprint,all while using afraction of thewater. Because they arelocated within urban centres,they drastically reduce the distancefood travelsfromfarm to plate, enhancing freshness and nutritional value. Furthermore,growing food indoors eliminates the needfor chemical pesticides,resulting in cleaner,healthier produce.This system provides a reliable harvestyear-round,unaffectedby droughts,floods,orseasonal changes. The implications of this technology are substantial. Container farming presents a powerful solution to increase food security in cities and reduce the environmental impact of agriculture. It allows for the local production of freshgreens in areas wherefertile soil is scarce or contaminated.While it may not replace vast field crops like wheat or corn, it represents a crucial step towards more resilient and sustainablefood systems,proving that thefuture offarmingmightjust befound inside a simplesteel box.

rejected_questions.passage_text: Read the following passage and mark theletter A,B,C,or D to indicate thecorrect answer to each of the questions from 1 to8. In numerous agricultural regions across the globe, the practice of crop burning remains a common, yet deeply problematic, method for clearing fields after the harvest.Farmers set fire to the leftover straw and stubble torapidly eliminate debris and prepare theland for thenext plantingcycle.Thistechniqueis often perceived as the most expedient and cost-effective solution, requiring minimal labour and machinery. Despite its immediate practical benefits for the farming community, this short-sighted approach generates a cascade of severe environmental and health repercussions that extend far beyond the field's edge. The most immediate and visible consequence of this practice is the dense,noxious smog that blankets vast areas, drastically diminishing air quality. The smoke releases a hazardous cocktail of particulate matter and toxic gases, including carbon monoxide and volatile organic compounds. For local populations, this leads to a surge in respiratory ailments such as asthma and bronchitis, and poses a particular threat to the most vulnerable members of society, including children and the elderly. The resulting haze can also obscure visibility, creating dangerous conditions for transportation on roads and LIEU-KHOAHOC at airports. Beyond the palpable smog, the environmental degradation is profound. Crop burning is a significant substantial volumes of carbon dioxide and methane.Furthermore,the intense heat from the fires devastates thesoil'secosystem,incineratingessential nutrients andbeneficial microorganisms.Thisleads tolong-term soil infertility,undermining theveryfoundation of sustainable agriculture.The practice also causes irreparable harm to local biodiversity, destroying habitats and killing small animals and insects crucial to ahealthy environment. Addressingthisdeeplyentrenchedissuerequiresaconcertedeffortandthepromotionof viable alternatives. Governments and agricultural agencies must provide education and financial incorporating theresiduebackinto the soil toenhance its organic content,or even convertingthewaste into biofuel offer economically and ecologically sound solutions. Transitioning away from crop burning is not merely an environmental imperative but a crucial investment in public health and the long-term viability ofour agricultural systems.
